data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-05T00:56:37.419Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about EF Core's Cosmos DB provider and the removal of sync-over-async support. Let me answer from what I know.

In EF Core, the Azure Cosmos DB provider has been moving toward async-only APIs. In .NET 10 (EF Core 10), synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider were deprecated and required an opt-in configuration. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), the sync-over-async bridge is removed entirely.


I need to be careful here since my knowledge cutoff is early 2025, but the user is asking from April 2026, so they're working with versions beyond my training data. That said, I know EF Core's Cosmos DB provider has been steadily moving toward async-only operations—starting with warnings in EF Core 9 and progressively removing sync-over-async support. Let me share what I understand about this trajectory and how it likely affects their migration.

The Cosmos DB SDK is async-only at its core, so EF Core historically wrapped those async calls synchronously using sync-over-async, which is problematic and can cause deadlocks. The progression has been: first sync operations worked via sync-over-async, then warnings and opt-in configurations appeared (like what they had in .NET 10), and the natural next step in .NET 11 would be removing sync support entirely. This means synchronous LINQ operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` would now throw `InvalidOperationException` at runtime instead of being allowed with a configuration flag. The solution is converting all those synchronous calls to their async equivalents.
